risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A statistician from the University of California Davis, who was blinded to the intervention groups, generated a random allocation sequence at the level of the concession (extended family compound) using SAS V.9.3 (SAS Institute Inc, Cary, North Carolina, USA) to assign eligible children in the intervention cohort to one of four intervention groups." Comment: This explicitly describes a random allocation sequence generated with SAS by a blinded statistician, which is a proper random method and sufficient for Low risk.

_evidence.method_quote: A statistician from the University of California Davis, who was blinded to the intervention groups, generated a random allocation sequence at the level of the concession (extended family compound) using SAS V.9.3 (SAS Institute Inc, Cary, North Carolina, USA) to assign eligible children in the intervention cohort to one of four intervention groups.
_evidence.method_summary: A blinded statistician generated a random allocation sequence in SAS v9.3 at the concession level.
context_clues: stratified by health clinic affiliation, average population size, and distance from the paved road and Bobo-Dioulasso

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A statistician from the University of California Davis, who was blinded to the intervention groups, generated a random allocation sequence at the level of the concession (extended family compound) using SAS V.9.3 ... to assign eligible children ..." Comment: This describes sequence generation and that the statistician was blinded, but it does not explicitly state a recruiter-level concealment mechanism (e.g. central allocation, SNOSE, identical containers) preventing foreknowledge of the next assignment before enrollment. Therefore allocation concealment cannot be confirmed and is judged unclear.

_evidence.concealment_quote: A statistician from the University of California Davis, who was blinded to the intervention groups, generated a random allocation sequence at the level of the concession (extended family compound) using SAS V.9.3 (SAS Institute Inc, Cary, North Carolina, USA) to assign eligible children in the intervention cohort to one of four intervention groups. Every concession had a 1/8 chance of receiving one of the eight colour codes (two colours for each group to reinforce the blinding). During the trial, all participants, field staff, study statistician and investigators were blinded to the intervention groups.
_evidence.note: The article describes sequence generation and blinding/colour codes, but does not describe envelopes or another recruiter-level concealment procedure.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "During the trial, all participants, field staff, study statistician and investigators were blinded to the intervention groups. ... All SQ-LNS products had the same appearance, aroma and flavour, and the zinc and placebo dispersible tablets were identical in appearance and flavour." Comment: The study explicitly states that both participants and field staff/personnel were blinded, and describes matching placebo/active products to maintain masking. Although several outcomes were subjective (e.g. caregiver-reported morbidity), successful blinding makes performance bias unlikely.

_evidence.blinding_quote: The study design has been described in detail by Hess et al.21a The study was registered with the US National Institutes of Health as a clinical trial (http://www.ClinicalTrials.gov; NCT00944281). ... During the trial, all participants, field staff, study statistician and investigators were blinded to the intervention groups. ... SQ-LNS, zinc and placebo tablets were produced by Nutriset SAS (Malaunay, France). All SQ-LNS products had the same appearance, aroma and flavour, and the zinc and placebo dispersible tablets were identical in appearance and flavour.
_evidence.blinding_method: double-blind, placebo-controlled; all SQ-LNS products had the same appearance, aroma and flavour, and the zinc and placebo dispersible tablets were identical in appearance and flavour; two colours for each group to reinforce the blinding

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "During the trial, all participants, field staff, study statistician and investigators were blinded to the intervention groups." Comment: The outcome assessors were the field staff collecting the weekly morbidity history, and the paper explicitly states they were blinded. The main outcomes included caregiver-reported morbidity, but because the data collectors/assessors were blinded and the paper reports double-masked design with blinded investigators and field staff, knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence outcome measurement.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote:
The strengths of the study include the double-masked randomised design and the rigorous quality control of the morbidity data collection with weekly supervision and regular retraining of the data collection team.

A statistician from the University of California Davis, who was blinded to the intervention groups, generated a random allocation sequence at the level of the concession (extended family compound) using SAS V.9.3 (SAS Institute Inc, Cary, North Carolina, USA) to assign eligible children in the intervention cohort to one of four intervention groups. Every concession had a 1/8 chance of receiving one of the eight colour codes (two colours for each group to reinforce the blinding). During the trial, all participants, field staff, study statistician and investigators were blinded to the intervention groups.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The 71 children who were excluded from the final analyses because they provided less than 30 days of morbidity observations did not differ at baseline from those included in the analysis (data not shown). There were no differences in participation or reporting rates among the four intervention groups." Comment: The analysed morbidity outcome had very high follow-up (2435 enrolled; 2364 included in final analyses), with only 71/2435 excluded overall (~2.9%) and no meaningful arm imbalance (602→579, 613→593, 603→593, 617→599). The exclusion reason was insufficient morbidity observation time, not an outcome-related reason, and the paper states no differences in participation/reporting rates across groups. This is consistent with low risk of attrition bias for the analysed outcomes.

_evidence.notes: Final morbidity analyses included only children with at least 30 days of morbidity observations; 71 children were excluded from final analyses for insufficient morbidity observations. Numbers come from the morbidity outcome analysis, not the full enrolled cohort.
